quotes.verbatim_quote:
The diatonic scale is a sequence of 7 musical notes organized in half steps and whole steps.
    It is based on the 7 “natural” notes: C, D, E, F, G, A, B. The half steps are between the notes:
    B, C and E, F.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the foundational scale and locates the two natural half steps the rest of the book relies on.

quotes.verbatim_quote: • W-W-H-W-W-W-H • (W = whole tone / H = half tone).
quotes.why_load_bearing: The canonical interval formula used to build every major scale referenced later.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
We can start the scale from any note, but the sound of the sequence will not be the same,
    because the scale pattern will be changed. For example: The "C" major scale pattern is:
    -W - W - H - W - W - W - H, but if we start it from the second note (D) the pattern would
    change to - W - H - W - W - W - H - W.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Explains why accidentals are required to preserve the major pattern in other keys.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The natural minor scale, also called relative scale, is the major scale itself started from the
6th degree. Therefore, if we have a "C major" scale, the minor scale will be "A minor".
quotes.why_load_bearing: Establishes the relative-minor relationship the book uses throughout.

quotes.verbatim_quote: •W-H-W-W-H-T-T•
quotes.why_load_bearing: Gives the interval formula for the natural minor scale.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The chromatic scale consists of 12 half steps. In other words, it contains all 12 notes that

                                68
exist in western music (C, C#, D, D#, E, F, F#, G, G#, A, A#, B).
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the 12-note universe from which all intervals and scales are drawn.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Left Hand Fingers:
    • Index Finger = 1
    • Middle Finger = 2
    • Ring Finger = 3
    • Baby Finger (or Pinky) = 4
quotes.why_load_bearing: Establishes the finger numbering convention used in every chord diagram in the book.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
1) When there is a “X” on a string, it                     2) When there is a circle (Ο) on a string, it
    means that the string must be muted or                     means that the string is open.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the X and O symbols required to read every chord diagram.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
3) When needed, in order to not have to draw a giant fretboard, we use a number on the
                            46
    right side of it, that represents the exact fret to be used. Notice that we always use this
    number according to the closest ;inger from the guitar neck.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines how the book denotes higher-position chord shapes.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
when we use an arched line on many strings at        4668




once, it means that we must press the quantity of strings the arched line is showing us
using one 8inger. Usually we use the index to do that, however we have many examples
where we use other 8ingers. For these cases, instead using an arched line, we repeat the
number of the 8ingering to be used.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines barre notation and the repeated-number convention for non-index barres.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
5) For scales and single notes visualization on the fretboard, we only use interval numbers
over each string. Remember that intervals must be related to a chosen root, otherwise it

                     46
would be impossible to number them.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Establishes that scale diagrams show intervals (not fingers) relative to a root.

quotes.topic: Splitting the fretboard around the root
quotes.verbatim_quote:
The chosen Root (R) divides the
                                                                      Guitar Fretboard in two parts:
                                                                      Left Side & Right Side.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Introduces the left/right/central spatial framework used to organize intervals around the root.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Actually the guitar fretboard ends at the
                                                           12th fret, because the notes repeat in the
                   A#   D#      G#
1st Fret →    F    Bb   Eb      Ab     C       F           same order they appeared previously. In
                                                           other words, the 13th fret is the same as
                                                           the 1st and so on.
quotes.why_load_bearing: States the octave-repeat principle for fretboard memorization.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Step 1:
     Memorize the names of the open string notes.

     Step 2:
     Memorize the names of the natural notes on the 6th and 5th strings (Note: "natural note"
     means notes without any accidentals). Notes containing accidentals will consequently be
     visualized. Remember that the 1st and 6th strings are the same (E).

     Step 3:
     Notes belonging to the 4th, 3rd string can be visualized using the interval of octaves from
     the 5th and 6th strings.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Prescribes the book's specific octave-based method for learning fretboard notes.

quotes.topic: Second string via fourth string octave
quotes.verbatim_quote:
Step 4:
                                   68
     The second string notes can be visualized using an octave from the 4th string. So, before
     trying to visualize the 2nd string notes, make sure you know the 4th string notes very well.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Handles the B-string anomaly via a derived-octave shortcut from the 4th string.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
However, we must be careful when learning
     a scale, because if we memorize it only by shape without knowing the intervals, eventually we
     will have to come back and learn the intervals, otherwise it would be impossible to go to the
     next step. Therefore, both must be learned at the same time.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Core methodological rule: never learn shape without interval understanding.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The secret to visualizing the guitar
     fretboard is to learn everything having two guide points:
     a) guide note on the 6th string
     b) guide note on the 5th string
     That guide note is what we call ROOT or 1, and all the intervals we play will be related with
     it, even the most distant ones.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Names the book's two-guide-point system used for every interval and chord shape.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
a) MELODIC & HARMONIC INTERVALS : Melodic intervals are notes played one after the
     other, and harmonic intervals are notes played simultaneously.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Foundational distinction needed before any chord/voicing discussion.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
b) SIMPLE & COMPOUND INTERVALS : Simple intervals are intervals within one octave,
     and compound intervals are those separated by more than one octave.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the simple/compound boundary that justifies labels like M9, b9, M2 vs M9.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
d) DIATONIC & CHROMATIC INTERVALS : Diatonic intervals are those determined
by notes in the diatonic scale (major scale), and chromatic intervals are those determined by
modified notes of the diatonic scale.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Distinguishes scale-derived intervals from altered ones, prerequisite to chord alterations.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
f) ENHARMONIC INTERVALS: These are two notes notated or written differently, but with
the same sound.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines enharmonic equivalence used throughout chord-spelling decisions.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Knowing the intervals on paper is not enough. It is necessary to learn them by singing. In
order to make that easier, let's use some songs as references. The process is quite simple, we will
learn the beginning of each song that contains each one of the 12 ascending intervals, as well the
12 descending intervals. Therefore, there will be 24 songs (24 intervals).
quotes.why_load_bearing: Prescribes the song-reference ear-training method the book hangs on.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Ascending Intervals:
1) Minor Second = Isn't She Lovely (Stevie Wonder)
2) Major Second = Happy Birthday (P. Hill / M. J. Hill )
3) Minor Third = Sugar (Stanley Turrentine)
4) Major Third = I Can't Get Started (Vernon Duke)
5) Perfect Fourth = Someday My Prince Will Come (Frank Churchill)
6) Aug Fourth/Dim Fifth = Maria - West Side Story (L. Bernstein)
7) Perfect Fifth = Star Wars (John Williams)
8) Aug Fifth/ Minor Sixth = Manhã de Carnaval (L. Bonfá)
9) Major Sixth = Take the “A” Train (Billy Strayhorn)
10) Minor Seventh = Star Trek theme (M. Giacchino)
11) Major Seventh = Super Man (J. Williams)
12) Perfect Octave = Somewhere Over the Rainbow (Harold Arlen)
quotes.why_load_bearing: The book's complete reference table for ear-training each ascending interval.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Descending Intervals:         68
1) Minor Second = Stella by Starlight (Victor Young)
                     46
2) Major Second = Yesterday (Beatles)
3) Minor Third = Hey Jude (Beatles)
4) Major Third = Summertime (G. Gershwin)
5) Perfect Fourth = Softly as in a Morning Sunrise (Romberg/Hammerstein II)
6) Aug Fourth/ Dim Fifth = Black Sabbath (Black Sabbath)
7) Perfect Fifth = Flintstones Theme (H. S. Curtin)
8) Augmented Fifth/ Minor Sixth = Love Story Theme (Francis Lai)
9) Major Sixth = Man In the Mirror; chorus (Michael Jackson)
10) Minor Seventh = Watermelon Man (Herbie Hancock)
11) Major Seventh = I Love You (Cole Porter)
12) Perfect Octave = Willow Weep for Me (Ann Ronell)
quotes.why_load_bearing: The descending-interval companion reference for the ear-training method.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
1) Up a Minor 2nd = Down a Major 7th

     2) Up a Major 2nd = Down a Minor 7th

     3 Up a Minor 3rd = Down a Major 6th

     4) Up a Major 3rd = Down a Minor 6th

                                 68
     5) Up a Perfect 4th = Down a Perfect 5th


                        46
     6) Up a Augmented 4th (diminished 5th) = Down a Augmented 4th (no changes)

     7) Up a Perfect 5th = Down a Perfect 4th

     8) Up a Augmented 5th (minor 6th) = Down a Major 3rd

     9) Up a Major 6th = Down a Minor 3rd

     10) Up a Minor 7th = Down a Major 2nd

     11) Up a Major 7th = Down a Minor 2nd

     12) Up a Octave = Down a Octave ( no changes)
quotes.why_load_bearing: Codifies interval inversion, foundational for understanding chord inversions and voice leading.
